questions.question: What does the CBIGK mnemonic help you remember?
options: A. Airway adjuncts
B. Key chest pain assessment elements: Chest pain, Blood pressure, Irregular pulse, Glucose, Potassium
C. Shock stages
D. Stroke scale components
questions.rationale: CBIGK organizes the vital assessment elements for a chest pain patient: Chest pain characteristics, Blood pressure, Irregular pulse, Glucose, and Potassium.

questions.question: A patient with breast cancer on chemotherapy presents with dyspnea on exertion, JVD, muffled heart tones, and hypotension. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A. Pneumothorax
B. Cardiac tamponade
C. Simple anxiety
D. Asthma exacerbation
questions.rationale: JVD, muffled heart tones, and hypotension constitute Beck's triad, classic for cardiac tamponade — here likely from a chemotherapy-associated pericardial effusion.

questions.question: Which three findings make up Beck's triad?
options: A. Fever, tachycardia, hypotension
B. Jugular venous distention, muffled heart tones, hypotension
C. Wheeze, stridor, cyanosis
D. Chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea
questions.rationale: Beck's triad — JVD, muffled heart tones, and hypotension — indicates cardiac tamponade.

questions.question: Which ECG leads correspond to an inferior MI?
options: A. V1-V4
B. II, III, aVF
C. I, aVL
D. V5-V6
questions.rationale: Leads II, III, and aVF show changes in an inferior wall myocardial infarction.

questions.question: Which ECG leads correspond to an anterior MI?
options: A. II, III, aVF
B. V1-V4
C. aVR only
D. Lead I only
questions.rationale: Leads V1 through V4 show changes in an anterior wall myocardial infarction.

questions.question: A 12-lead ECG shows ST depression in V1-V4 without obvious elevation elsewhere. What should you consider?
options: A. A completely normal ECG
B. Posterior MI, which can appear as a mirror-image depression pattern in these leads
C. Right bundle branch block only
D. This pattern always means the patient is fine
questions.rationale: Posterior MI classically shows as ST depression in V1-V4 (mirror-image of the elevation that would appear on true posterior leads) rather than obvious elevation — a commonly missed pattern.

questions.question: When should you consider obtaining a right-sided ECG?
options: A. Only in trauma patients
B. When the standard 12-lead is non-diagnostic, especially with suspected inferior MI
C. Never — it adds no useful information
D. Only after hospital arrival
questions.rationale: A right-sided ECG helps identify right ventricular involvement when the standard 12-lead is non-diagnostic and inferior MI is suspected, directly changing fluid/drug management.

questions.question: Why is right ventricular infarction clinically important to identify?
options: A. It has no treatment implications
B. These patients are often preload-dependent and can become severely hypotensive with nitrates or other vasodilators
C. It always requires immediate surgery
D. It only matters for documentation
questions.rationale: Right ventricular infarction patients depend on adequate preload; giving nitrates or other vasodilators can precipitate dangerous hypotension.

questions.question: How does cardiac tamponade differ from pneumothorax on physical exam?
options: A. They present identically
B. Tamponade shows muffled heart tones with JVD and hypotension; pneumothorax shows absent breath sounds instead
C. Pneumothorax always causes JVD and tamponade never does
D. Neither causes any exam findings
questions.rationale: Tamponade's hallmark is muffled heart tones with JVD and hypotension (Beck's triad); pneumothorax instead shows absent/diminished breath sounds on the affected side — a key distinguishing feature.

questions.question: Why is glucose included in the CBIGK chest pain assessment mnemonic?
options: A. It is irrelevant to cardiac presentations
B. Glucose abnormalities can mimic or complicate cardiac presentations, changing management
C. It only matters for pediatric patients
D. Glucose testing replaces the need for an ECG
questions.rationale: Glucose abnormalities (hypo/hyperglycemia) can present with cardiac-like symptoms or complicate an actual cardiac event, so checking it is part of a thorough chest pain assessment.

questions.question: Why does an irregular pulse matter during a chest pain assessment?
options: A. It has no clinical significance
B. It may indicate a dysrhythmia complicating the cardiac event and affecting treatment decisions
C. It only matters in pediatric patients
D. Irregular pulses are always benign
questions.rationale: An irregular pulse can signal a dysrhythmia (e.g., atrial fibrillation, ectopy) that changes both the differential and the immediate management plan.

questions.question: A patient with suspected right ventricular MI and borderline blood pressure is being considered for nitroglycerin. What is the concern?
options: A. No concern — nitrates are always safe in MI
B. Nitrates can cause severe hypotension in preload-dependent right ventricular infarction
C. Nitrates are only dangerous in anterior MI
D. Nitrates have no effect on blood pressure
questions.rationale: Because right ventricular infarction patients depend on adequate preload to maintain cardiac output, nitrates (which reduce preload) can precipitate dangerous hypotension.

questions.question: A patient presents with sudden dyspnea. Which finding would point AWAY from cardiac tamponade and toward pneumothorax instead?
options: A. Muffled heart tones
B. Absent breath sounds on one side
C. Jugular venous distention
D. Hypotension
questions.rationale: Absent/diminished breath sounds point to pneumothorax, whereas tamponade's hallmark exam findings are muffled heart tones, JVD, and hypotension (Beck's triad).

questions.question: What is the practical value of systematically building a chest pain/dyspnea differential rather than anchoring on the most common cause?
options: A. It wastes time in the field
B. It protects against missing a less common but immediately dangerous diagnosis, such as tamponade or PE
C. It is only useful for documentation, not patient care
D. It has no practical value
questions.rationale: Because chest pain and dyspnea share overlapping presentations across several dangerous diagnoses (tamponade, PE, pneumothorax, MI), a systematic differential approach catches conditions that anchoring on the most common cause would miss.
